tiles.name: Coyoacán
tiles.funFact: Frida Kahlo was born here in 1907, and her Casa Azul museum, opened in 1958, now draws over 800,000 visitors per year.

tiles.name: Xochimilco
tiles.funFact: A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987, Xochimilco preserves 170 km of ancient canals and chinampas dating back to 1200 AD.

tiles.name: Línea 1
tiles.funFact: Opened September 4, 1969, this 18.8 km line has 20 stations, each with a unique pictographic icon so riders can navigate without reading.

tiles.name: Roma Norte
tiles.funFact: Developed in the early 1900s and heavily damaged in the 1985 earthquake, Roma Norte was revitalized in the 2000s into one of the city's top cultural hubs.

tiles.name: Condesa
tiles.funFact: Built in the 1920s around the former Hipódromo de la Condesa racetrack, Parque México still traces the original oval track shape.

tiles.name: Polanco
tiles.funFact: Developed in the 1930s–40s and named after a 17th-century friar, Polanco hosts more museums per square kilometer than almost any neighborhood in the Americas.

tiles.name: San Ángel
tiles.funFact: The famous Bazar Sábado has run every Saturday since 1960 in the 17th-century Casa del Risco, making it one of Mexico's longest-running art markets.

tiles.name: CFE
tiles.funFact: Founded on August 14, 1937, CFE serves over 46 million customers and is one of the largest electric utilities in the Americas.

tiles.name: Chapultepec
tiles.funFact: Its name means 'Grasshopper Hill' in Nahuatl, and this 686-hectare park has been a sacred site since at least the 13th century.

tiles.name: Bosque de Chapultepec
tiles.funFact: At 686 hectares (twice the size of Central Park), the Bosque contains 9 museums, a zoo founded in 1924, and ahuehuete trees over 700 years old.

tiles.name: Línea 2
tiles.funFact: Opened August 1, 1970, this 23.4 km line runs beneath key landmarks, and its construction unearthed Aztec artifacts now displayed in the stations.

tiles.name: Teotihuacán
tiles.funFact: Built between 100 BC and 250 AD, the 65-meter-tall Pyramid of the Sun once anchored a city of over 125,000 residents, making it the 6th largest in the ancient world.

tiles.name: Templo Mayor
tiles.funFact: Discovered on February 21, 1978 when electrical workers struck a massive stone disc, the excavation has yielded over 7,000 Aztec artifacts from this once 60-meter-tall temple.

tiles.name: Zócalo
tiles.funFact: Officially the Plaza de la Constitución, this 46,800 m² square has been the center of Mexican public life since the Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was founded in 1325.

tiles.name: Palacio Nacional
tiles.funFact: Built on the site of Moctezuma II's palace starting in 1522, Diego Rivera painted his epic murals across its walls from 1929 to 1951.

tiles.name: Bellas Artes
tiles.funFact: Construction began in 1904 but took 30 years to complete; its famous Tiffany glass curtain weighs 24 tons and depicts the Valley of Mexico volcanoes.

tiles.name: Reforma
tiles.funFact: Commissioned by Emperor Maximilian in 1864 as 'Paseo de la Emperatriz,' this 15 km boulevard was renamed after the Reform War and is now Mexico City's main artery.

tiles.name: Línea 3
tiles.funFact: Opened November 20, 1970, this 23.6 km line with 21 stations is one of the system's busiest, carrying over 1 million riders daily.

tiles.name: Ángel de la Independencia
tiles.funFact: Inaugurated on September 16, 1910 for the centennial of independence, the gold-plated angel statue stands 6.7 meters tall atop a 36-meter column.

tiles.name: Zona Rosa
tiles.funFact: Named in the 1960s by artist José Luis Cuevas, it became Mexico's first openly LGBTQ+ neighborhood in the 1980s and home to a thriving Koreatown since the 1990s.

tiles.name: Agua de México
tiles.funFact: Mexico City pumps 40% of its water from the Cutzamala system 127 km away; over-extraction from aquifers causes the city to sink 5–10 cm per year.

tiles.name: Santa Fe
tiles.funFact: Transformed in the 1990s from what was once Latin America's largest garbage dump into a gleaming business district with over 10,000 corporate offices.

tiles.name: Museo Frida Kahlo
tiles.funFact: La Casa Azul opened as a museum in 1958, four years after Frida's death in 1954, and now receives over 800,000 visitors annually.

tiles.name: Museo Soumaya
tiles.funFact: Opened in 2011 with a façade of 16,000 aluminum hexagons, it houses 66,000 artworks including the largest Rodin collection outside France.

tiles.name: Antropología
tiles.funFact: Opened September 17, 1964, this museum holds the 24-ton Aztec Sun Stone and receives over 2 million visitors per year.

tiles.name: Línea 12
tiles.funFact: Opened October 30, 2012, this 24.5 km golden-colored line with 20 stations is the newest addition to Mexico City's metro network.

tiles.name: Torre Latinoamericana
tiles.funFact: Completed in 1956 at 44 stories (182 m), it survived the devastating 8.0-magnitude 1985 earthquake without structural damage thanks to its deep-pile floating foundation.

tiles.name: Castillo de Chapultepec
tiles.funFact: Built between 1785 and 1864, it is the only royal castle in the Americas; Emperor Maximilian and Empress Carlota lived here from 1864 to 1867.
